title: Revisit Daily Life in China in 1917 Through Footage Enhanced and Colorized by AI
description:
in Film, Travel |   	May 25th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Even for AmerôÙiôÙcans, keepôÙing up with the geopoôÙlitôÙiôÙcal entanôÙgleôÙments of the Uni
body:
in Film, Travel |   	May 25th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Even for AmerôÙiôÙcans, keepôÙing up with the geopoôÙlitôÙiôÙcal entanôÙgleôÙments of the UnitôÙed States has nevôÙer been an easy task. More than a cenôÙtuôÙry ago, just a few months after their counôÙtry got involved in whatãs now known as World War I, they got word that the milôÙiôÙtary of a disôÙtant nation had joined their side: ChiôÙna, whose image would have been both opaque and forôÙbidôÙdingôÙly vast. A dozen years beforeô theyãd even heard the name Pearl S. Buck,ô what impresôÙsions of that counôÙtry they had would have come from scatôÙtered sources like post-OpiôÙum Wars misôÙsionôÙary pubôÙliôÙcaôÙtions, newsôÙpaôÙper covôÙerôÙage of comôÙpliôÙcatôÙed events likeô the BoxôÙer RebelôÙlion and the fall of the Qing dynasty, and silent-film genre stereoôÙtypes. (PerôÙhaps the rare readôÙer got ahold of John ThomôÙsonãs Through ChiôÙna with a CamôÙera.) Most could live a lifeôÙtime withôÙout a glimpse of ãthe real ChiôÙna.ã
By the end of 1917, howôÙevôÙer, ãthere were at least 10 docôÙuôÙmenôÙtaries availôÙable to satôÙisôÙfy curiosôÙiôÙty about Americaãs new ally in the Far East,ã accordôÙing to the NationôÙal Film PreserôÙvaôÙtion FounôÙdaôÙtion. Most were shorts that played alongôÙside feaôÙtures, but A Trip Through ChiôÙna was difôÙferôÙent. At least five years in the makôÙing, ãthe docôÙuôÙmenôÙtary was the brainôÙchild of BenôÙjamin BrodôÙsky, a wideôÙly travôÙeled RussôÙian-born busiôÙnessôÙman who claimed to speak 11 lanôÙguages. AccordôÙing to a 1912 MovôÙing PicôÙture World proôÙfile, the young entreôÙpreôÙneur had moved to ChiôÙna from San FranôÙcisôÙco after the 1906 EarthôÙquake and set up shop as a film exhibitor. Soon, as the AmerôÙiôÙcan repôÙreôÙsenôÙtaôÙtive of VariôÙety Film Exchange, he had a hand in disôÙtriôÙbôÙuôÙtion and by 1909 branched into film proôÙducôÙtion in ShangôÙhai and Hong Kong. While jugôÙgling busiôÙness interôÙests, he filmed his travôÙels,ã all of which took place not just before ChiôÙnaãs ecoôÙnomôÙic rise, butô before even the ComôÙmuôÙnist RevôÙoôÙluôÙtion.


	

BrodôÙsky brought 20,000 feet of negôÙaôÙtives with him back to San FranôÙcisôÙco, evenôÙtuôÙalôÙly cutôÙting it down to ten reels, which would have run around one hour and 50 minôÙutes. Of this feaôÙture-length travôÙelôÙogue film only cerôÙtain secôÙtions surôÙvive, but you can see them enhanced and colôÙorized with artiôÙfiôÙcial intelôÙliôÙgence in the video at the top of the post. (Some of an un-enhanced black-and-white print appears just above.) Bear in mind that colôÙors you see are not, of course, the colôÙors BrodôÙsky would have seen; thereãs also some disôÙcusôÙsion about whether the AI renôÙdered cerôÙtain comôÙplexôÙions unreôÙalôÙisôÙtiôÙcalôÙly dark for the regions in which he shot these scenes. For ChiôÙna is quite a diverse place, not just in regionôÙal landôÙscapes, cliôÙmates, and culôÙtures, but also in the faces of its peoôÙple: someôÙthing many WestôÙernôÙers wouldôÙnãt have guessed in the nineôÙteen-tens ã and for that matôÙter, someôÙthing a fair few of them donãt realôÙize even today.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
Behold the PhoôÙtographs of John ThomôÙson, the First WestôÙern PhoôÙtogôÙraôÙphôÙer to TravôÙel WideôÙly Through ChiôÙna (1870s)
A Trip Around the World in 1900: See Restored Footage ShowôÙing Life in New York, LonôÙdon, India, Japan, ChiôÙna & Beyond
Footage of Cities Around the World in the 1890s: LonôÙdon, Tokyo, New York, Venice, Moscow & More
Watch Life on the Streets of Tokyo in Footage RecordôÙed in 1913: Caught Between the TraôÙdiôÙtionôÙal and the ModôÙern
A Trip Through New York City in 1911: VinôÙtage Video of NYC Gets ColôÙorized & Revived with ArtiôÙfiôÙcial IntelôÙliôÙgence
The PhoôÙto That TrigôÙgered Chinaãs DisôÙasôÙtrous CulôÙturôÙal RevôÙoôÙluôÙtion (1966)
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Who Would Be King of the United States If George Washington Had Become a Monarch?
description:
in History |   	May 25th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The young George WashôÙingôÙton may nevôÙer have hacked up his fatherãs cherôÙry tree and refu
body:
in History |   	May 25th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The young George WashôÙingôÙton may nevôÙer have hacked up his fatherãs cherôÙry tree and refused to lie about it, but his life nevôÙerôÙtheôÙless offers plenôÙty of deeds both virôÙtuôÙous and adeôÙquateôÙly docôÙuôÙmentôÙed. It was no small thing, for instance, to refuse to seek a third term as the first PresôÙiôÙdent of the UnitôÙed States of AmerôÙiôÙca ã much less to exchange that title for ãKing of the UnitôÙed States of AmerôÙiôÙca.ã As every enthuôÙsiôÙast of AmerôÙiôÙcan hisôÙtoôÙry knows, this set the preceôÙdent, only once vioôÙlatôÙed and thereôÙafter writôÙten into law, of a two-term limôÙit. But as every enthuôÙsiôÙast of alterôÙnate AmerôÙiôÙcan hisôÙtoôÙry has wonôÙdered, what would have hapôÙpened had WashôÙingôÙton become king? And if the U.S. monarôÙchy had remained intact for the past 227 years, who would rule it today?
In the UseôÙfulCharts video above, Matt BakôÙer explains a series of difôÙferôÙent sucôÙcesôÙsion sceôÙnarôÙios. While none is highôÙly plauôÙsiôÙble in itself, they togethôÙer give an idea of the lines along which AmerôÙiôÙcan monarôÙchiôÙcal hisôÙtoôÙry could have played out, at least assumôÙing that every othôÙer event played out exactôÙly the same way as it has in our realôÙiôÙty.
One of the first comôÙpliôÙcatôÙing facôÙtors is that WashôÙingôÙton himôÙself had no bioôÙlogôÙiôÙcal descenôÙdants. GivôÙen that, we can trace down a theôÙoôÙretôÙiôÙcal royôÙal linôÙeage startôÙing with his adoptôÙed son, born from his wife Marthaãs first marôÙriage; with the nephew he selectôÙed as the priôÙmaôÙry heir of his estate; or with the senior-most heir of his father (ownôÙer of the notionôÙal cherôÙry tree). Not that any of those major paths through the chart of WashôÙingôÙtonãs indiôÙrect descenôÙdants is necôÙesôÙsarôÙiôÙly straightôÙforôÙward either.
The whole matôÙter seems at least as comôÙpliôÙcatôÙed as figôÙurôÙing out who would be the Roman emperôÙor if Rome had nevôÙer fallôÙen, an exerôÙcise BakôÙer works through in anothôÙer UseôÙfulCharts video preôÙviôÙousôÙly feaôÙtured here on Open CulôÙture. The posôÙsiôÙbilôÙiôÙties for the AmerôÙiôÙcan monarch in 2026 come down to King Robert III, or Robert E. Lee V (and great-great grandôÙson of Robert E. Lee); Queen BrynôÙda, or BrynôÙda Hansen; King Richard, or Richard WashôÙingôÙton; and King LarôÙry II, or Lawrence ShaffnôÙer, the descenôÙdant of George WashôÙingôÙtonãs nephew Bushrod. BakôÙer finds that ShaffnôÙer is the most conôÙvincôÙing canôÙdiôÙdate for the job, which is hard to deny. Even apart from the relôÙeôÙvant familôÙial, politôÙiôÙcal, and legal facôÙtors, conôÙsidôÙer that name again. King LarôÙry: apart from the title, how much more AmerôÙiôÙcan could it posôÙsiôÙbly sound?
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:

How George WashôÙingôÙton Became PresôÙiôÙdent of the UnitôÙed States: It Was WeirdôÙer Than You Think

title: Hear Robert Johnsonãs ãCome On in My Kitchenã in Remarkably Restored Audio, Taken from a Rare Test Pressing
description:
in Music |   	May 26th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Robert JohnôÙson died at just 27 years old, some say as a conôÙseôÙquence of sellôÙing his soul
body:
in Music |   	May 26th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Robert JohnôÙson died at just 27 years old, some say as a conôÙseôÙquence of sellôÙing his soul to the devôÙil at a crossôÙroads. But before his time came, he manôÙaged to record 29 songs, a scant body of work that nevôÙerôÙtheôÙless secured his artisôÙtic immorôÙtalôÙiôÙty as one of the most influôÙenôÙtial blues musiôÙcians of all time. Itãs unforôÙtuôÙnate that his recordôÙings, all of them made between 1936 and 1937 in less-than-ideôÙal stuôÙdio conôÙdiôÙtions even for the time, leave someôÙthing to be desired in the audio qualôÙiôÙty departôÙment. But now, some 90 years latôÙer, sound restorôÙer Nick DelôÙlow has been uploadôÙing relôÙaôÙtiveôÙly crisp digôÙiôÙtized ãtest pressôÙingsã of JohnôÙsonãs songs to YouTube: last month, for examôÙple, we feaôÙtured one of ãCross Road Bluesã here on Open CulôÙture.
In the video above, youãll find a simôÙiôÙlarôÙly highôÙer-qualôÙiôÙty verôÙsion of ãCome On in My Kitchen,ã a song acknowlôÙedged as an earôÙly demonôÙstraôÙtion of the young JohnôÙsonãs othôÙerôÙworldôÙly musiôÙcal powôÙer. You may notice that the title labels this parôÙticôÙuôÙlar recordôÙing as ãtake one.ã JohnôÙson also recordôÙed a much difôÙferôÙent secôÙond take, which his label Vocalion Records released in 1937, posôÙsiôÙbly because it soundôÙed less mournôÙful and thus ã accordôÙing to record-indusôÙtry logôÙic ã more viable as a hit.
Though take one now seems to be regardôÙed as the ãtrueã renôÙdiôÙtion of the song by his seriôÙous enthuôÙsiôÙasts, the pubôÙlic didôÙnãt get to hear it until 1961, when it was includôÙed on the comôÙpiôÙlaôÙtion King of the Delta Blues Singers that did more than any othôÙer release to win JohnôÙson his posthuôÙmous fan base.
It is, admitôÙtedôÙly, not easy to imagôÙine the first take of ãCome On in My Kitchenã sweepôÙing the dance halls, even with this sound qualôÙiôÙty much improved from the verôÙsion on King of the Delta Blues Singers. But the reaôÙsons JohnôÙsonãs music has endured so long have less to do with his abilôÙiôÙty to get a crowd movôÙing than with his comôÙbiôÙnaôÙtion of underôÙstatôÙed virôÙtuôÙosôÙiôÙty and preterôÙnatôÙurôÙal-soundôÙing abilôÙiôÙty to reach into genôÙuineôÙly hauntôÙing emoôÙtionôÙal realms. Like many canonôÙiôÙcal singer-songôÙwritôÙers who died young, he seems always to be and remain someôÙhow oldôÙer than us, his lisôÙtenôÙers, even as we reach (and indeed pass) midôÙdle age. OccaôÙsionôÙalôÙly, the release of nevôÙer-before-heard recordôÙings or pressôÙings reveals the true edge of immaôÙtuôÙriôÙty in such figôÙures; with JohnôÙson, it only deepôÙens his legôÙend.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:

Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: The Myth of Sisyphus Wonderfully Animated in an Oscar-Nominated Short Film (1974)
description:
in Film |   	May 26th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Even if you donãt know the myth by name, you know the stoôÙry. In Greek mytholôÙoôÙgy,ô SisyôÙphus
body:
in Film |   	May 26th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Even if you donãt know the myth by name, you know the stoôÙry. In Greek mytholôÙoôÙgy,ô SisyôÙphus, King of Corinth, was punôÙished ãfor his self-aggranôÙdizôÙing craftiôÙness and deceitôÙfulôÙness by being forced to roll an immense boulôÙder up a hill, only to watch it roll back down, repeatôÙing this action for eterôÙniôÙty.ãô In modôÙern times, thisô stoôÙry inspired Albert Camus to write ãThe Myth of SisyôÙphus,ã an essay where he famousôÙly introôÙduced his conôÙcept of the ãabsurdã and idenôÙtiôÙfied SisyôÙphus as the absurd hero. And it proôÙvidôÙed the creôÙative mateôÙrôÙiôÙal for a breathôÙtakôÙingôÙly good aniôÙmaôÙtion creôÙatôÙedô by MarôÙcell Jankovics in 1974. The film, notes the annoôÙtaôÙtion that accomôÙpaôÙnies the aniôÙmaôÙtion on YouTube, is ãpreôÙsentôÙed in a sinôÙgle, unbroôÙken shot, conôÙsistôÙing of a dynamôÙic line drawôÙing of SisyôÙphus, the stone, and the mounôÙtainôÙside.ã FitôÙtingôÙly, Jankovicsã litôÙtle masôÙterôÙpiece was nomôÙiôÙnatôÙed for the Best AniôÙmatôÙed Short Film at the 48th AcadôÙeôÙmy Awards.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2015.
If you would like to supôÙport the misôÙsion of Open CulôÙture, conôÙsidôÙer makôÙingô a donaôÙtion to our site. Itãs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions will help us conôÙtinôÙue proôÙvidôÙing the best free culôÙturôÙal and eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal mateôÙriôÙals to learnôÙers everyôÙwhere. You can conôÙtribute through PayôÙPal, PatreôÙon, and VenôÙmo (@openculture). Thanks!

title: Ava Roth Collaborates with Insects to Create 'Kintsu-Bee' Ceramic Vessels
description: In Ava Rothãs sculpture practice, a finished piece is the result of careful planning and tending, but the outcome can only be predicted so much. W
body:
In Ava Rothãs sculpture practice, a finished piece is the result of careful planning and tending, but the outcome can only be predicted so much. Whether creating wooden frameworks or organic embroideries, the artist leaves it to bees to create the ultimate form.
Roth has long invited the honeycomb-building insects to play a role in her work, often adding wonderfully bulbous constructions that occasionally disrupt the artistãs carefully placed boundaries. Wooden pieces are mandala-like and take on the quality of low reliefs once the bees have done their part. Recently, she leapt into the three-dimensional realm via ceramics and a time-honored tradition of repair in her series Kintsu-Bee.

The new body of work is a play on the Japanese word kintsugi, which describes a traditional method of repairing ceramics with metallic lacquer. The process embraces the nature of the breakage itself, mending the vessel yet highlighting the cracks as a way of embracing the objectãs history rather than trying to camouflage it. In Rothãs iteration, bees are invited to reconstruct the missing parts, guided around forms to create the missing handle of a mug or fill in the fissures of a dinner plate.
ãMirroring the philosophy of kintsugi, the unique architecture of the comb acts both as a restorative measure and as a visual memory of the past,ã says a statement.ô ãWhen extracted, the delicacy and complexity of the composite objectsãhalf human and half insectãtell a story not just of human violence but of the earthãs capacity for repair.ã
See more on Rothãs Instagram.

schema.headline: Ava Roth Collaborates with Insects to Create 'Kintsu-Bee' Ceramic Vessels

title: Eight London Underground Restrooms Get Vibrant Refurbs with Illustrated Tiles
description: Home to some of Londonãs most iconic landmarks, the City of Westminster sees around 25 million of tourists every year. Add that number to resident
body:
Home to some of Londonãs most iconic landmarks, the City of Westminster sees around 25 million of tourists every year. Add that number to residents and professionals who transit through central London daily, and weãre talking lots of people using the Underground, a.k.a. the Tube. Fortunately for travelers, eight stations are getting restroom upgrades with a big assist from Hugh Broughton Architects.
When the Victorians established the first underground train network in the mid-to-late 1800s, they incorporated arched details and tile work that is preserved today, often augmented with contemporary nods to the cityãs history and culture. Opened in 1863 along the Metropolitan Line, Baker Street is one of the oldest stations. In a design convention seen throughout the Underground, decorated tiles added later feature a silhouette of Sherlock Holmesãa tribute to the characterãs fictional address on the stationãs titular street.

Tile designs are richly embedded throughout the system. An architect named Leslie Green is known for a slew of deep red tile facades on more than a dozen central stations. Some interiors are covered practically top to bottom with the material, with the location names and ãWay Outã signs made entirely from ceramic. Bethnal Green station features fantastic relief tiles of London icons, and a few years ago, a design student named Jeffrey Miller even repurposed the Tubeãs own waste into Art Nouveau-style tiles modeled on historic versions.
Hugh Broughton Architectsã redesign continues this tradition, but it also serves the practical purpose of making these spaces safer, more inclusive, and more accessible. ãThere is a clear demand for public toilets across London, and many councils struggle with the costs surrounding maintenance and issues around antisocial behaviour,ã says a statement. ãTo address this issue, the council is creating a series of inclusive facilities that demonstrate high-quality design and enshrine as much civic pride as the Victorians displayed when they first started looking at a proper sanitation system for the general public in the 19th century.ã
The facilities at Victoria Embankment, Parliament Street, Piccadilly Circus, Green Park, Carnaby Street, Westminster Pier, Covent Garden, and Leicester Square are all getting vibrant makeovers. The tiles incorporate designs by artist and illustrator James Lambert, who interprets the distinctive character of each location through historical motifs and iconography. Among numerous playful references, youãll find patterns consisting of the Kingãs Guards in their iconically tall, black, furry helmets and a tribute to George John Vulliamyãs giant sphinxes that guard Cleopatraãs Needle on the Embankment.
The restrooms are being refurbished in succession. Victoria Street opened in February 2025, followed by Parliament Street, and now Piccadilly Circus and Green Park. The next will be Carnaby Street and Westminster Bridge. Follow updates on the firmãs Instagram.

schema.headline: Eight London Underground Restrooms Get Vibrant Refurbs with Illustrated Tiles

title: Vibrant Miniature Dioramas by Shannon Taylor Burst from Vintage Compacts
description: From dusky woodland scenes to fantastical landscapes populated by mythical creatures, Shannon Taylorãs vibrant dioramas spring to life inside vint
body:
From dusky woodland scenes to fantastical landscapes populated by mythical creatures, Shannon Taylorãs vibrant dioramas spring to life inside vintage cosmetic compacts. Sourced from flea markets, vintage shops, friends, and anywhere else they might appear, the vessels themselves are often brimming with character, but they let on little about what resides inside. Tiny mermaids, unicorns, and a range of flora and fauna made with watercolor on paper beckon us into a Polly Pocket-sized realm.
In addition to the inclusion of her work in Common Waters at Arch Enemy Arts, Taylor is preparing for a solo exhibition titled Minor Mending at Modern Eden in San Francisco, which opens on August 6. She also curated a show at the Oakland Museum of California titled Fairyland @ 75: A Legacy of Magic, organized on behalf of Childrenãs Fairyland, where she is Director of Art and Restoration. The show continues through the end of the summer. See more on Taylorãs Instagram.


Exterior of the diorama shown above

Exterior of the diorama shown above



A diorama in progress

schema.headline: Vibrant Miniature Dioramas by Shannon Taylor Burst from Vintage Compacts
